summary: Joel Spolsky dismisses Microsoft's .NET announcement as worse than vaporware, arguing that even the conceptual substance behind it is lacking. He expresses frustration that the tech industry and media have been 'duped' into treating .NET as revolutionary. The post is a brief teaser directing readers to his longer article 'Microsoft Goes Bonkers' for the full argument. He also notes he's been on vacation, explaining the gap in posts.
key_points: Microsoft .NET is characterized as worse than vaporware — even the 'vapor' (the conceptual vision) is absent
The tech industry has been uncritically credulous in treating .NET as revolutionary
Joel directs readers to his longer-form critique 'Microsoft Goes Bonkers' for the detailed argument
money_quotes: It's not Microsoft .NET per se that gets me all riled up, it's that everyone has been duped into thinking that this is some kind of revolutionary new thing when it's really one level worse than vaporware: even the vapor isn't there.
key_insight: Microsoft's .NET announcement was worse than vaporware because it lacked even a coherent vision behind the hype, yet the industry accepted it uncritically as revolutionary.
